description: Engaged but resistant prospect — not excessive cooperation

vars.transcript:
REP: We can reduce your incident response time by 60%.
PROSPECT: That sounds good on paper. What does that look like in practice for a team our size?
REP: Automated playbooks handle the first hour — your team only gets involved when human judgment is needed.
PROSPECT: My team is skeptical of automation. We had a bad experience where an automated rule blocked legitimate traffic for four hours.
REP: That's a fair concern. Our rules are behavior-based, not signature-based — much lower false positive rate. What was the tool that caused the outage?
PROSPECT: I'd rather not name them but it was a well-known vendor. I'd need to see your false positive data before I'd trust this in production.
REP: Absolutely. I can share that — our median false positive rate is 0.3% across healthcare customers.

description: Zero friction throughout — prospect answers everything and volunteers info

vars.transcript:
REP: What's your current security setup?
PROSPECT: We use a basic SIEM. It doesn't do behavioral detection, there are compliance gaps, and our team is understaffed. We have $30k available and I can approve it without going to anyone.
REP: What's your timeline?
PROSPECT: As soon as possible — Q1 ideally. We want to move fast.
REP: Any concerns about switching vendors?
PROSPECT: No, none at all. Whatever you recommend we'll do.
REP: We'd need a 2-week implementation window.
PROSPECT: Perfect, we can do that. Should I loop in our CFO?
REP: Up to you.
PROSPECT: I'll just sign it myself, I have the authority. Send over the contract.

description: Warm prospect who still has real concerns — not excessive cooperation

vars.transcript:
REP: Thanks for taking the call, Sarah.
PROSPECT: Of course, I've been looking forward to it. I did some research on you.
REP: What did you find?
PROSPECT: Good things honestly. But I want to be upfront — we have an existing contract with our current vendor through next year. That's the main hurdle.
REP: Tell me about that contract.
PROSPECT: 18 months left, $10k remaining on it. If we switched we'd be eating that cost.
REP: Would that be a dealbreaker or a negotiating point?
PROSPECT: Depends on what you can offer to offset it. I'm not writing off $10k just because your demo is impressive.

description: Short conversation — not enough data to flag

vars.transcript:
REP: Hi, is this a good time?
PROSPECT: Actually I only have five minutes — my next meeting just got moved up.
REP: Understood. Can I send you some materials and we set a proper time?
PROSPECT: Sure, send them over. My email is sarah@meridianhealth.com.
REP: Will do. Talk soon.

description: Long call — raises four concerns, folds on each after one sentence

vars.transcript:
REP: What's driving the evaluation?
PROSPECT: Compliance pressure. SOC 2 audit in Q2.
REP: What's your current tooling?
PROSPECT: Basic SIEM. Honestly it's not cutting it.
REP: Have you looked at other vendors?
PROSPECT: A couple. You came highly recommended.
REP: Our platform is $35k annually.
PROSPECT: That's a bit more than I expected.
REP: It includes the full compliance module.
PROSPECT: Oh, that makes sense then.
REP: What about implementation timing?
PROSPECT: We're running a cloud migration right now, so capacity is tight.
REP: Our implementation is mostly hands-off for your team.
PROSPECT: Perfect, that solves it.
REP: Any concerns about switching away from your current vendor?
PROSPECT: I mean, there's always a transition risk.
REP: We have a dedicated migration team.
PROSPECT: Great, that covers it.
REP: Do you need executive sign-off?
PROSPECT: My CTO usually reviews these.
REP: We can put together an exec summary.
PROSPECT: That would be helpful. I think she'd be fine with it.
REP: So are we good to move to next steps?
PROSPECT: Yes, let's do it. Send over the contract.
